headline: Federal ROAD Act in HUD rulemaking, Pennsylvania's delinquent-tax notification reform now in effect, and New York's pocket-listing and deed-protection bills target fall passage.
paragraphs: No bill status changes appeared in yesterday's data refresh — summer recess continues to hold most tracked legislatures static — so today's focus is implementation and positioning. The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act (P.L. 119-101, signed July 11) is the most consequential recent action: it expands FHA multifamily loan limits, widens HOME program eligibility, streamlines environmental review for residential construction and rehab, and eliminates the permanent-chassis requirement for manufactured-home loans. HUD has not yet published implementing guidance; lenders with pending FHA-backed multifamily or manufactured-housing deals should hold their underwriting assumptions until guidance drops. Pennsylvania's HB96 (Act No. 27 of 2026, signed July 12) is the other recent enactment deserving immediate attention — it amends the Real Estate Tax Sale Law to allow property owners to designate a representative to receive delinquent-tax notices in parallel with the owner of record, materially reducing the risk of surprise tax sales on portfolios where borrower contact information differs from the deed holder. Servicers and lenders managing Pennsylvania-based loans should update their default-notice workflows accordingly.

New York's pre-fall calendar features several bills with clear market consequences lined up for early session votes. A10679 — which would require prompt public marketing of all listed residential properties and allow off-MLS or pocket-listing marketing only with the seller's written, informed consent after a standardized state disclosure — cleared the Senate and was returned to the Assembly on June 1, with the Senate companion S10274 consolidated into A10679B; a final-passage run is in reach for the fall session. A09554, the Deed Protection Act, passed the Assembly in May and awaits the Senate: it would bar foreclosure on any mortgage the lender knows — or should know — rests on a deed procured through fraud or forgery, adding title-diligence and origination-compliance implications for any New York lender. A11490 (algorithmic pricing ban) and S08612 (good-cause-eviction technical fix) remain in committee but are credible fall targets; passage of S08612 in particular would resolve ongoing litigation and underwriting ambiguity on covered multifamily portfolios.

Florida's constitutional property-tax overhaul — signed June 16 as HJR 1-F following the Sixth Special Session — is now a voter-ratification campaign rather than a legislative question. If approved in November, the measure tightens the annual assessment-increase cap, raises the homestead exemption, and limits how counties and municipalities can levy ad valorem taxes; lenders underwriting Florida income-producing properties should model both the 'amendment passes' and 'amendment fails' scenarios before committing to long-horizon projections. At the federal level, the workforce housing tax credit (HB8626, Ways and Means) and LIHTC expansion (HB2725, Ways and Means) remain the two most important pending tax-equity vehicles for affordable and workforce housing development, with their fate increasingly entangled with broader Q4 tax-reform negotiations. The foreign-buyer restriction cluster — now spanning six bills across five committees — continues to advance slowly on parallel tracks; comprehensive beneficial-ownership screening on any deal with potential foreign-linked capital remains the prudent standard regardless of near-term enactment prospects.
